title: 9 Herbal Creams For Migraine
intro_desc: Herbal creams can be a great way to relieve migraine pain, especially when combined with other treatments. But did you know that herbal teas can also provide relief? This is because herbs have anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties that can help to calm the nervous system and reduce migraine symptoms. For example, Lavandula angustifolia, also known as lavender, contains a compound called linalool, which has a soothing effect on the mind and body. Drinking lavender tea can help to reduce anxiety and promote relaxation, making it easier to fall asleep and wake up feeling refreshed.  Another herb that can help to relieve migraine is Valeriana officinalis, or valerian root. This herb has been used for centuries to treat insomnia and anxiety, and has also been shown to have a sedative effect on the nervous system. Drinking valerian root tea can help to calm the mind and body, reducing migraine symptoms and promoting a good night's sleep.  Ginger, or Zingiber officinale, is another herb that has been used for centuries to treat nausea and pain. Drinking ginger tea can help to reduce inflammation and relieve migraine symptoms, making it easier to get through the day. Ginger also has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce pain and swelling in the body.  Using herbal teas to relieve migraine can bring many benefits to your life. Not only can they help to reduce pain and symptoms, but they can also promote relaxation and improve sleep quality. By incorporating herbal teas into your daily routine, you may find that you experience fewer migraine episodes and are able to live a more active and fulfilling life.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia creams contains the bioactive constituents linalool and linalyl acetate. These compounds are known for their ability to reduce inflammation and ease muscle spasms, which are common triggers for migraines. The calming effects of linalool and linalyl acetate can also help to relax tense muscles and improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted by migraine attacks. Additionally, lavender's antispasmodic properties can help to reduce the severity and frequency of migraine headaches by relaxing blood vessels and improving blood flow to the brain. The combination of these properties makes Lavandula angustifolia creams a promising natural remedy for migraine sufferers.
remedies.image_alt: Lavandula angustifolia herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of shea butter, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of dried Lavandula angustifolia, 2 tablespoons of jojoba oil, and 2 teaspoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt coconut oil, shea butter, and beeswax in a double boiler or a microwave-safe bowl in 10-second increments, stirring between each interval, until melted and smooth.
Add dried Lavandula angustifolia to a small bowl and pour jojoba oil over it. Let it steep for 2-3 hours or overnight. Strain the oil through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl.
Combine the melted mixture, strained Lavandula angustifolia oil, and vitamin E oil in a clean bowl. Stir well to combine.
Pour the mixture into a silicone candy mold or a small tin container. Let it cool and solidify at room temperature. Once set, store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 2 weeks.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis creams contains valepotriates, isovaleramide, and valerenic acid as its active constituents. These compounds have a sedative and anti-inflammatory effect on the body, which can help to alleviate migraine symptoms. Valepotriates have a calming effect on the nervous system, reducing muscle tension and pain. The anti-inflammatory properties of valerenic acid can help to reduce swelling and pain in the blood vessels that surround the brain, which are often affected during a migraine. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, Valeriana officinalis creams can help to provide relief from migraine symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Valeriana officinalis herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of carrier oil (coconut or jojoba oil), 2 tablespoons of Valeriana officinalis root extract, and a small saucepan.
Melt 1/2 cup of carrier oil in the saucepan over low heat. Remove from heat when melted.
Add 2 tablespoons of Valeriana officinalis root extract to the melted oil. Stir well to combine.
Let the mixture cool down to room temperature. Then, stir it every 10-15 minutes for 2 hours to ensure it cools evenly.
Once cooled, transfer the mixture to a clean container and store it in the refrigerator. Use as needed for migraine relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale creams contains a compound called gingerol, which is a potent anti-inflammatory. Gingerol works by blocking the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that trigger pain and inflammation. The cream also contains shogaol, another active compound that helps to relax blood vessels and improve blood flow to the brain. This can help to reduce the severity and frequency of migraines by reducing inflammation and relieving pain. By applying Zingiber officinale cream to the skin, the active compounds can be absorbed and reach the brain, where they can help to alleviate migraine symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil, 10 drops of ginger essential oil.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Once melted, remove from heat and stir in vitamin E oil and ginger essential oil.
Pour the mixture into small containers or a glass jar and let it cool until solidified.
Apply a small amount to the affected area as needed, ideally 2-3 times a day, to help relieve migraine symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Foeniculum vulgare creams contains the active constituent fenchone, which has a sedative effect on the nervous system. It also contains estragole, a compound that helps to relax tense muscles and improve blood flow to the brain. Foeniculum vulgare creams have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce swelling and pain in the head and neck area, common symptoms of migraines. The anethole in these creams has a calming effect on the stomach, which can help to alleviate nausea and vomiting associated with migraines. By relaxing the nervous system, improving blood flow, reducing inflammation, and calming the stomach, Foeniculum vulgare creams can help to alleviate migraine symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Foeniculum vulgare herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of Foeniculum vulgare oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of coconut oil, and 2 drops of lavender essential oil.
Melt Foeniculum vulgare oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add coconut oil to the melted oil and stir until combined.
Remove from heat and let it cool for 5-7 minutes, then add lavender essential oil and stir well.
Pour the mixture into a small container and let it cool completely before using as a migraine cream.

remedies.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita creams contains active constituents like menthol, menthone, and limonene. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, which help to reduce pain and swelling. Menthol, in particular, can help to relax muscles and improve blood flow, which can be beneficial in relieving migraine symptoms. The analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects of Mentha x piperita creams can also help to reduce the frequency and severity of migraines. The essential oils in Mentha x piperita creams can also help to reduce nausea and vomiting associated with migraines.
remedies.image_alt: Mentha x piperita herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried peppermint leaves, 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil, and 2 tablespoons of lavender oil.
Heat the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler over low heat until the beeswax is melted.
Add the dried peppermint leaves to the melted mixture and let it steep for 10-15 minutes. Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth to remove the peppermint leaves.
Add the vitamin E oil and lavender oil to the mixture and stir well. Pour the mixture into a container and let it cool.
Once the mixture has cooled and solidified, use a spoon to scoop out a small amount and apply it to your skin as needed to relieve migraine symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis creams contains rosmarinic acid, a powerful antioxidant that helps reduce inflammation in the brain. This antioxidant also has a direct effect on the blood vessels, causing them to relax and widen, which can help alleviate migraine pain. The anti-inflammatory properties of rosmarinic acid also help reduce the production of pro-inflammatory chemicals in the brain, further contributing to its pain-relieving effects. Additionally, Rosmarinus officinalis creams contain carnosic acid, which has been shown to inhibit the activity of enzymes involved in the production of pain-causing chemicals in the brain. By applying Rosmarinus officinalis creams topically, rosmarinic acid and carnosic acid can be absorbed through the skin and into the bloodstream, providing relief from migraine pain.
remedies.image_alt: Rosmarinus officinalis herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of shea butter, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil, and 2 tablespoons of dried Rosmarinus officinalis leaves.
Melt coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of boiling water.
Add shea butter and stir until melted. Remove from heat and let cool slightly.
Add vitamin E oil and dried Rosmarinus officinalis leaves to the mixture. Stir well and let it steep for 30 minutes to an hour.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a bowl. Pour the cream into clean containers and let it cool before use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Achillea millefolium creams contains compounds like sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which have anti-inflammatory and pain-relieving properties. These sesquiterpene lactones, such as achillol and alantolactone, inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes, reducing swelling and pain in the brain. The flavonoids, including quercetin and luteolin, have antioxidant properties that neutralize free radicals and reduce oxidative stress, which can contribute to migraine symptoms. The phenolic acids, like caffeic acid, have analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects, further alleviating migraine pain. By reducing inflammation, pain, and oxidative stress, Achillea millefolium creams may help alleviate migraine symptoms and provide relief.
remedies.image_alt: Achillea millefolium herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of dried Achillea millefolium, 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of beeswax, and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt the coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl over a pot of simmering water.
Add the dried Achillea millefolium to the melted oil and wax mixture. Let it steep for 2-3 hours or overnight.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl. Discard the solids.
Add the vitamin E oil to the mixture and stir well. Pour the mixture into a tin or a jar and let it cool and solidify.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis creams contains rosmarinic acid, apigenin, and linalool, which are bioactive compounds that help alleviate migraine symptoms. Rosmarinic acid has anti-inflammatory properties, reducing swelling and pain in blood vessels. Apigenin and linalool possess sedative and antispasmodic properties, calming the nervous system and relaxing tense muscles. These properties work together to reduce the frequency and severity of migraine attacks. By reducing inflammation and promoting relaxation, Melissa officinalis creams can help provide relief from migraine symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Melissa officinalis herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of Melissa officinalis oil, 1/4 cup of coconut oil, 1 tablespoon of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, and 2 teaspoons of vitamin E oil.
Melt coconut oil, beeswax, and shea butter in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add Melissa officinalis oil and vitamin E oil to the melted mixture. Stir well to combine.
Remove the mixture from heat and let it cool until it reaches a temperature of around 120°F to 130°F.
Pour the mixture into a container and let it cool completely before use. Store in a cool, dark place.

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginkgo biloba creams contains flavonoids and terpenoids as its bioactive constituents. Flavonoids, such as quercetin and kaempferol, have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help reduce migraine pain and inflammation. Terpenoids, including ginkgolides and bilobalide, have a potent inhibitory effect on platelet-activating factor (PAF), a chemical that causes blood vessels to constrict and worsen migraine symptoms. By blocking PAF, ginkgo biloba creams can help improve blood flow to the brain, reducing migraine severity and frequency. Regular use of ginkgo biloba creams may also help reduce the frequency of migraines by improving blood vessel health and reducing inflammation.
remedies.image_alt: Ginkgo biloba herbal creams for migraine
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of coconut oil, 1/2 cup of shea butter, 2 tablespoons of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of Ginkgo biloba extract, and 2 drops of lavender essential oil.
Melt coconut oil and shea butter in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water.
Add beeswax to the melted mixture and stir until it's fully incorporated.
Remove the mixture from the heat and let it cool slightly. Add Ginkgo biloba extract and lavender essential oil, and stir well.
Pour the mixture into a small tin or jar and let it cool and harden. Store the Ginkgo biloba cream in a cool, dry place.

faq_prevent: Drinking herbal tea may help prevent migraines from forming. Some herbs, like feverfew and ginger, have anti-inflammatory properties that can ease pain and reduce inflammation. Peppermint tea can also relax tense muscles and calm the mind, which may help prevent migraines. These teas are often consumed regularly to see a positive effect.
faq_safe: While herbal teas can be a helpful migraine relief, drinking them daily might not be suitable for everyone. Some herbs, like feverfew, can be beneficial in small amounts but may cause side effects or interact with medications when consumed regularly. It's best to start with moderate use and see how your body reacts.
faq_timeline: Herbal teas can start helping with migraines within a few days to a week. Some people notice a difference after just 24 to 48 hours. The teas work by reducing inflammation, relaxing muscles, and improving sleep, which can all contribute to fewer migraine episodes.
faq_time: If you get migraines, try drinking herbal tea when you wake up in the morning or early afternoon. This can help prevent a migraine from happening. Some herbal teas, like feverfew or ginger, have properties that can ease migraine symptoms. Drinking them before a migraine starts may help reduce its severity.
faq_interaction: Herbal teas can potentially interact with prescription medications for migraine. Some herbs like feverfew, butterbur, and ginger may slow blood clotting or affect blood pressure, making them a concern with blood thinners or medications that lower blood pressure. This could worsen migraine symptoms or increase the risk of bleeding.
faq_pregnant: If you're pregnant and have migraines, it's best to limit herbal teas. Some herbs can cause problems for you and your baby. For example, feverfew can thin your blood and increase the risk of bleeding. Ginger is often used for nausea, but it's usually okay in small amounts. Always check the ingredients and talk to someone if you're unsure.
faq_breastfeeding: Herbal teas can be a helpful way to manage migraines, but it's essential to be cautious when breastfeeding. Some herbal teas, like feverfew and ginger, are safe for breastfeeding mothers, but others, such as pennyroyal and blue cohosh, may not be suitable. It's best to choose teas that are known to be safe during breastfeeding.
title_2: 9 Herbal Creams for Women to Ease Migraine Symptoms
